---
title: Morphokinetic properties of mesenchymal stem cells as a predictor of differentiation potential
author: "Y3948024"
output:
  bookdown::pdf_document2:
    toc: false
    fig_caption: true
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \captionsetup{labelfont=bf}
  - \usepackage{float}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
library(lme4)
library(tidyverse)
library(lmerTest)
library(MuMIn)
library(kableExtra)
```

# Abstract

Human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) collected from the femoral head have a range of clinical applications, from the union of non-healing bone fractures to the treatment of diabetic neuropathy. hMSCs are a highly heterogenous population with varying differential potential, and the identification of high potency cells is critical in ensuring viable transplant. Current procedures to quantify hMSC differential potential are destructive, highly invasive, or require animal models; previous research demonstrate, however, that cytoskeletal architecture and gross cellular morphology may be a predictor of potency. Prior studies have primarily been qualitative or limited in capacity; in this study, we utilise high-throughput Livecyte ptychographic quantitative phase imaging to quantify 9 morphokinetic characteristics of two clones of hMSCs. Potency was assessed via alkaline phosphatase activity as a marker of early osteogenic differentiation. Our results suggest that high-potency cells exhibited a spindle morphology with parallel F-actin architecture, while low-potency cells exhibited a broader and flatter cobblestone-like morphology, with decreased exploratory movement and a dispersed F-actin network. Combined with machine learning trained on large datasets of characterised cells, this approach may offer fast and minimally-invasive quantification of stem cell potency, improving the clinical efficacy of hMSC therapies in regenerative medicine.

\textbf{Word count: n (https://wordcounter.net)}


# Introduction

Human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) are multipotent non-hematopoietic cells found in bone marrow stroma that are capable of self-renewal and differentiation into a range of mesodermal tissues, such as bone, fat, cartilage, and muscle (Aldahmash \textit{et al.}, 2012). Due to their high culturability and potency, they are increasingly used in the clinical treatment of a variety of conditions, including non-healing bone fractures (Trombi \textit{et al.}, 2016), diabetic neuropathy (Liu and Tang, 2016), and diverse brain injuries (Hsuan \textit{et al.}, 2016). However, hMSC populations are highly heterogenous: within a single bone marrow aspirate, clonal subpopulations exhibit varying differentiation potential (Costa \textit{et al.}, 2021). This variation imposes a significant barrier to hMSC-based therapies as the potency of a patient-derived aspirate is unpredictable, limiting the treatment's efficacy. For example, Trombi \textit{et al.} (2016) show that clinical trials for fracture nonunion using hMSCs have shown mixed outcomes, noting the distinct heterogeneity of bone marrow as a primary factor. Thus, identifying clonal lines with specific potency before committing to extensive differentiation assays could significantly improve the long-term prognosis of hMSC therapies.

Currently, definitive markers of osteogenic potency are destructive (Smith \textit{et al.}, 2025) or require mouse models, such as in Ectopic Bone Formation assays (Scott \textit{et al.}, 2012). However, recent research by Yourek \textit{et al.} (2007) used fluorescence imaging to reveal hMSC cytoskeletal remodelling in response to osteoinduction, with cells increasing in F-actin stress fibre density and spreading area. This was further explored by Matsuoka \textit{et al.} (2013), who demonstrated that morphological features obtained by phase-contrast imaging could predict osteogenic differentiation with high accuracy, while Anconelli \textit{et al.} (2025) showed that hMSC morphology is a predictor of age-related senescence. Bertolo \textit{et al.} (2015) further identified that in-vitro motility parameters, such as cell migration speed, can distinguish multipotent from unipotent hMSC subpopulations.

Despite this research, the relationship between morphokinetic properties of hMSCs and their potency has primarily been explored using conventional phase-contrast microscopy, limiting temporal resolution and sample size. Studies that do use high-throughput imaging primarily focus on senescence, while our utilisation of Human Telomerase Reverse Transcriptase (hTERT) eliminates this as a variable by immortalising the clones, shifting the focus onto potency alone. Using ptychographic quantitative phase imaging (QPI) via the Livecyte microscope (Phasefocus, Sheffield, UK; Marrison \textit{et al.}, 2013), we analysed 6 morphological and 3 kinetic parameters of two clonal lines of hMSCs. An osteogenesis assay was then used to determine the differentiation potential of each clone, linking the Livecyte data to hMSC potency. Differences were then statistically examined to explore whether morphokinetic features alone are sufficient to separate the two clones in a multivariate space.


# Approach

This study compared the morphokinetic properties and osteogenic potential of two hMSC clonal lines (A and B) derived from the femoral head of a single patient in order to exclude inter-donor variability, immortalised via hTERT to ensure a stable phenotype without replicative senescence (Adam \textit{et al.}, 2025), isolating potency as the independent variable. Livecyte ptychographic QPI (Phasefocus, Sheffield, UK; Marrison \textit{et al.}, 2013) was used to quantify 9 morphokinetic features across three technical replicates per clone, imaging every 23 minutes over 4 days and yielding 7,916 single-cell observations, which was reduced to 664 (clone A) and 612 (clone B) after debris filtration. Fluorescence microscopy with Alexa-594-conjugated phalloidin and DAPI was used to visualise F-actin architecture and nuclear morphology, as cytoskeletal reorganisation is associated with osteogenesis (Khan \textit{et al.}, 2020). Osteogenic differentiation was assessed via alkaline phosphatase (ALP) spectrophotometry, a marker of early osteogenic commitment. Cells were first cultured in basal expansion medium to ensure any pre-existing osteogenesis was completed, followed by 8 days in osteoinductive medium- a duration sufficient to capture changes in ALP activity (Matsuoka \textit{et al.}, 2013). The osteoinductive medium contained dexamethasone, $\beta$- glycerophosphate, and L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (Yourek \textit{et al.}, 2007). Dexamethasone promotes RUNX2-mediated ALP transcription, while $\beta$-glycerophosphate and L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate support collagen I synthesis and matrix mineralisation. As data were hierarchical, linear mixed models were used with clone as a fixed effect and replicate as a random effect; principal component analysis was performed to assess multivariate separation. Statistical analyses were performed in R (version 4.4.1; R Core Team, 2026).


# Results

## Cell morphology

hMSC morphology was examined through Livecyte QPI, allowing for the analysis of 9 cell morphology parameters. Fluorescence microscopy was used to capture the diverse cytoskeletal morphologies of clones A and B (Figure 1). At 40x magnification, clone A cells displayed a smaller, elongated, spindle-like morphology (Figure 1A), while clone B cells showed a larger, more cobblestone-like morphology (Figure 1C). At 100x magnification, clone A exhibited parallel F-actin fibres aligned along the long axis of the cell (Figure 1B), while clone B showed a more radially-organised F-actin architecture (Figure 1D). Both clones exhibited intact nuclear morphology, as indicated by DAPI staining, suggesting that the cells were healthy and viable.

```{r figure1, out.width = "75%", fig.pos = "H", fig.cap = "\\textbf{Immunofluorescence microscopy shows heterogenous actin filament architecture of clone A and clone B hMSCs.} \\newline Immunofluorescence imaging at 40x and 100x magnification. Alexa-594-conjugated phalloidin (red, F-actin) and DAPI (blue, nuclear dsDNA). Scale bars: 100 $\\mu$m (40x), 50 $\\mu$m (100x)."}
knitr::include_graphics("project/figures/fluorescence.png")
```

The differences in cell morphology are reflected in Figure 2, which shows the quantitative and statistical analysis of cell volume, sphericity, radius, and aspect ratio (Figure 2A, B, C, and D, respectively). Thickness and sphericity were directly correlated ($\rho$ = 1), so only sphericity is reported; likewise with dry mass and volume. No significant difference in cell volume was discovered between clone A and clone B (median 1754.03 $\mu$m$^{3}$ vs 1824.57 $\mu$m$^{3}$, $p$ = 0.275), however clone A had a significantly higher sphericity (median 0.26 vs 0.15, $p$ < $2.2*10^{-16}$) and aspect ratio (median 3.06 vs 1.8, $p$ = 0.000286) than clone B. Clone A also had a significantly smaller radius than clone B (20.49 $\mu$m vs 27.81 $\mu$m, $p$ = 0.0000693), which is consistent with the cobblestone morphology of clone B observed in the fluorescence images. Although clone A appeared more spindle-like in Figure 1, its higher sphericity indicates a more compact three-dimensional profile: clone A cells were taller and narrower, whereas clone B cells were flatter and more spread across the substrate. These findings suggest that both clones of hMSC have distinct morphological properties.

```{r figure2, out.width = "50%", fig.pos = "H", fig.cap = "\\textbf{Morphological characterisation of clone A and clone B} \\newline Column scatter plots showing the distribution of (A) cell volume ($\\mu$m$^{3}$), (B) sphericity, (C) radius ($\\mu$m), and (D) aspect ratio of clone A and clone B. Individual points represent single cells. Diamonds indicate replicate means. Box plots show gross distribution: horizontal black bar shows $50^{th}$ percentile; box extends from the $25^{th}$ to the $75^{th}$ percentile, denoting interquartile range (IQR); whiskers extend Q $\\pm$ 1.5 $\\times$ IQR. Statistical comparisons performed using a linear mixed model, with clone as a fixed effect and replicate as a random effect, with Benjamini-Hochberg correction. $n_A$ = 664, $n_B$ = 612 across 3 technical replicates. ns = non-significant, * $p$ < 0.05, ** $p$ < 0.01, *** $p$ < 0.001, **** $p$ < 0.0001. Full summary statistics provided in Table 1."}
knitr::include_graphics('project/figures/morphology/morphology_figure.png')
```

## Cell motility

Cell motility was assessed through automated Livecyte tracking, with an image taken every 23 minutes for a total of 4 days. This allowed for the analysis of several movement parameters, including mean cell speed (Figure 3A), final displacement (Figure 3B), and total path length (Figure 3C). No significant difference in mean cell speed (median 0.33 $\mu$m min$^{-1}$ vs 0.27 $\mu$m min$^{-1}$, $p$ = 0.0807) or total path length (220 $\mu$m vs 199 $\mu$m, $p$ = 0.347) was found, however clone A had a significantly higher final displacement (130 $\mu$m vs 91 $\mu$m, $p$ = 0.00837). This is supported by the cell movement plot (Figure 3D), which shows that the tracks of clone A cells were less clustered and extended further from the origin. MSD analysis (Figure 3E) showed that both clones exhibited super-diffusive behaviour on a log-log scale across all time lags ($\tau$, from 1.5 to 96h in ~92-minute increments), with clone A having a higher $\alpha$ value than clone B (1.28 vs 1.17); this indicates that clone A cells migrated in a more exploratory and persistent manner, whereas clone B cells exhibited more convoluted, non-directional movement.

```{r figure3, out.width = "50%", fig.pos = "H", fig.cap = "\\textbf{Motility characterisation of clone A and clone B} \\newline Column scatter plots showing the distribution of (A) mean speed ($\\mu$m min$^{-1}$), (B) final displacement ($\\mu$m), and (C) total path length ($\\mu$m) of clone A and clone B. Individual data points represent single cells. Diamonds indicate replicate means. Box plots overlay gross distribution: horizontal black bar shows 50$^{th}$ percentile (median), box extends from the 25$^{th}$ to the 75$^{th}$ percentile, denoting interquartile range (IQR). Whiskers extend Q $\\pm$ 1.5 $\\times$ IQR. Statistical comparisons performed using a linear mixed model, with clone as a fixed effect and replicate as a random effect, with Benjamini-Hochberg correction. (D) Cell movement plot of each clone, starting points normalised to the origin; grey points show final cell position; n = 50 for each clone. (E) Mean Squared Displacement as a function of time interval ($\\tau$, from 1.5 to 96h in ~92-minute increments); lines represent the clone average, shaded regions show $\\pm$ 1 SE. ns = non-significant, * $p$ < 0.05, ** $p$ < 0.01, *** $p$ < 0.001, **** $p$ < 0.0001. Full summary statistics provided in Table 1."}
knitr::include_graphics('project/figures/movement/movement_figure.png')
```

## Multivariate analysis

### Linear mixed model

To assess how the morphokinetic features collected from the Livecyte microscope were different between clone A and clone B, a linear mixed model (LMM) was fitted for each feature, with clone as a fixed effect and replicate nested within clone as a random effect. The results of these models are summarised in Table 1. A forest plot of the LMM estimates (Figure 4) showed that the largest effects of clone were on final displacement, aspect ratio, mean thickness, radius, and sphericity, all of which were statistically significant after Benjamini-Hochberg correction (p < 0.01). Volume, dry mass, mean speed, and total path length did not differ significantly between clones. This is reflected in the low Marginal R$^2$ values for these parameters, which represents the proportion of variance explained by the fixed effect of clone. Intraclass correlation coefficients were low across all features (ICC < 0.05), suggesting that inter-replicate variability was low compared to inter-clone variability; this indicates that the observed differences are likely to be reproducible across technical replicates.

```{r figure4, out.width = "75%", fig.pos = "H", fig.cap = "\\textbf{Linear mixed model forest plot of 9 morphokinetic features} \\newline Points represent LMM estimates of the effect of clone (clone A vs clone B) on each feature, expressed in SD units. Error bars show 95% Wald confidence intervals. A positive estimate indicates that clone A has a higher value of the feature than clone B, and vice versa. ns = non-significant, * $p$ < 0.05, ** $p$ < 0.01, *** $p$ < 0.001, **** $p$ < 0.0001. Full summary statistics provided in Table 1."}
knitr::include_graphics('project/figures/lmm_results_plot.png')
```

```{r table1_code, include=FALSE}
source("project/scripts/3.linear_mixed_models.R")

report_table <- report_table |>
  slice(match(c("Total Path Length", "Final Displacement", "Volume", 
                "Radius", "Mean Thickness", "Sphericity", 
                "Aspect Ratio", "Dry Mass", "Mean Speed"), Feature))
```

```{r table1, echo=FALSE}
knitr::kable(report_table, 
             caption = "Statistical summary of clone A and B morphokinetic features",
             booktabs = TRUE) |>
  kable_styling(font_size = 9, 
                latex_options = c("hold_position", "scale_down")) |>
  footnote(general = c(
    "Estimates are from linear mixed models with clone as a fixed effect and replicate nested within clone as a random effect, expressed in SD units.",
    "95\\\\% CI: Wald confidence interval. Degrees of Freedom: Satterthwaite denominator degrees of freedom.",
    "P-values are Benjamini-Hochberg adjusted",
    "ICC: Intraclass Correlation Coefficient for replicate. R²m: Marginal R².",
    "Where p was calculated to be 0, it is reported as the R precision limit of 2.2e-16"
  ))
```

\newpage

### Principal component analysis

Principal component analysis was performed on the nine standardised features to visualise the multivariate separation between clones (Figure 5). PC1 and PC2 together explained 63% of the total variance. PC1 was most strongly associated with morphological features, such as volume, radius, thickness, and sphericity, while PC2 was more broadly associated with both morphological and motility features, such as volume, dry mass, total path length, and final displacement (Figure 5B). The PCA plot (Figure 5A) showed that the two clones form distinct clusters in the PC1-PC2 space, with PC1 being the primary separator, indicating that the morphological differences between the clones are a major source of variance in the data, and that these differences are statistically sufficient to separate the clones in a multivariate space ($\Delta$ PC1 and $\Delta$ PC2: p < 0.0001). These overall findings suggest that the morphokinetic properties of the cells are generally distinct between the two clones, and that these properties may be useful for predicting hMSC differentiation potential.

```{r figure5, out.width = "75%", fig.pos = "H", fig.cap = "\\textbf{Principal component analysis reveals that clone A and clone B form statistically distinct clusters} \\newline (A) PCA score plot of PC2 on PC1 for clone A (red) and clone B (blue). Each point represents a single cell; triangles represent clone centroids. Ellipses show 95% confidence regions. Both PC1 and PC2 scores differed significantly between clones (**** $p$ < 0.0001, Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test). (B) Loadings heatmap of 9 morphokinetic parameters. n$_A$ = 664, n$_B$ = 612 across 3 technical replicates. Full summary statistics are provided in Table 2."}
knitr::include_graphics('project/figures/PCA/PCA_figure.png')
```

```{r table2_code, include=FALSE}
source("project/scripts/4.principle_component_analysis.R")

pca_table <- as.data.frame(pca_result$rotation[, 1:9]) |>
  rownames_to_column("Feature") |>
  mutate(Feature = recode(Feature,
    "total_path_length" = "Total Path Length",
    "final_displacement" = "Final Displacement",
    "volume" = "Volume",
    "radius" = "Radius",
    "mean.thickness" = "Mean Thickness",
    "sphericity" = "Sphericity",
    "length.to.width.ratio" = "Aspect Ratio",
    "dry.mass" = "Dry Mass",
    "mean.speed" = "Mean Speed"
  )) |>
  mutate(across(c(PC1, PC2, PC3, PC4, PC5, PC6, PC7, PC8, PC9), ~ round(.x, 2))) |>
  bind_rows(tibble(
    Feature = c("Eigenvalue", "% Variance", "Cumulative %"),
    PC1 = c(round(pca_result$sdev[1]^2, 2),
            round(pve[1] * 100, 1),
            round(cumsum(pve)[1] * 100, 1)),
    PC2 = c(round(pca_result$sdev[2]^2, 2),
            round(pve[2] * 100, 1),
            round(cumsum(pve)[2] * 100, 1)),
    PC3 = c(round(pca_result$sdev[3]^2, 2),
            round(pve[3] * 100, 1),
            round(cumsum(pve)[3] * 100, 1)),
    PC4 = c(round(pca_result$sdev[4]^2, 2),
            round(pve[4] * 100, 1),
            round(cumsum(pve)[4] * 100, 1)),
    PC5 = c(round(pca_result$sdev[5]^2, 2),
            round(pve[5] * 100, 1),
            round(cumsum(pve)[5] * 100, 1)),
    PC6 = c(round(pca_result$sdev[6]^2, 2),
            round(pve[6] * 100, 1),
            round(cumsum(pve)[6] * 100, 1)),
    PC7 = c(round(pca_result$sdev[7]^2, 2),
            round(pve[7] * 100, 1),
            round(cumsum(pve)[7] * 100, 1)),
    PC8 = c(round(pca_result$sdev[8]^2, 2),
            round(pve[8] * 100, 1),
            round(cumsum(pve)[8] * 100, 1)),
    PC9 = c(round(pca_result$sdev[9]^2, 2),
            round(pve[9] * 100, 1),
            round(cumsum(pve)[9] * 100, 1))
  ))
```

```{r table2, echo=FALSE}
knitr::kable(pca_table,
             caption = "Principal component analysis of clone A and clone B morphokinetic features.",
             booktabs = TRUE) |>
  kable_styling(font_size = 9,
                latex_options = c("hold_position")) |>
  pack_rows("Loadings", 1, 9) |>
  pack_rows("Variance", 10, 12) |>
  footnote(general = c(
    "Loadings represent the contribution of each feature to the principal component.",
    "Only PC1 and PC2 are shown (63\\\\% cumulative variance)."
  ))
```

### Osteogenic differentiation

To assess the osteogenic differentiation potential of the two clones, cells were cultured in basal medium and osteoinductive medium for 8 days, and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity was measured via spectrophotometry as a marker of osteogenic differentiation (Figure 6). Concentration of DNA ($\mu$g mL$^{-1}$) was measured to control for cell number. ALP activity (A405) increased in both clones, with clone A showing a higher ALP signal after 8 days in osteogenic medium than clone B, with respect to cell number (mean $\Delta$ALP absorbance 0.367 AU vs 0.171 AU, $p$ = 0.1). Despite the low statistical significance due to the small sample size (n = 3), the change suggests that clone A had a higher osteogenic differentiation potential than clone B. Both clones showed increased DNA concentration over time, confirming that cells survived and proliferated on the new medium under both basal and osteogenic conditions.

```{r figure6, out.width = "75%", fig.pos = "H", fig.cap = "\\textbf{Alkaline phosphatase activity of clone A and clone B differed during osteogenic differentiation.} \\newline (Left y-axis; grey lines) Alkaline phosphatase activity measured via absorbance at 405nm (AU). Each line represents a technical replicate (n = 3). (Right y-axis; grey bars) Mean cell number measured via DNA concentration ($\\mu$g ml$^{-1}$). Measurements were taken at day 0, day 8 without osteogenic medium, and day 8 in osteogenic medium. "}
```


# Discussion

This study used fluorescence microscopy and ptychographic QPI to examine the morphology and motility of two clonal lines of human mesenchymal stem cells that differed in potency. The results revealed that clone A and B exhibited significantly different morphological and movement characteristics, particularly in sphericity, radius, thickness, aspect ratio, and final displacement. PCA showed that these differences are sufficient to separate the two clones in a multivariate feature space.

It should be noted that morphokinetic analysis and ALP activity were limited to three technical replicates per clone/condition, resulting in low statistical power. While the direction of the effect shows that cells with a spindle-like morphology had an increased ALP activity, biological replicates from multiple donors would be required to confirm statistical significance of increased osteogenic potential.

Our findings are generally consistent with existing literature: Yourek \textit{et al.} (2007) report that hMSCs transition from parallel stress fibres and a spindle-like morphology to a "crisscrossed" pattern with a flatter morphology after osteoinduction with dex. Under one interpretation, clone A corresponds to the pre-differentiation state described by Yourek \textit{et al.}, while clone B's radial actin architecture resembles the post-induction morphology. If so, this would indicate osteogenic rather than adipogenic or chondrogenic commitment, as both pre-adipocytes and pre-chondrocytes exhibit low spreading area with depolymerised or cortically-organised actin, respectively (Ambriz, De Lanerolle and Ambrosio, 2018), which is inconsistent with clone B's phenotype. These morphological changes are also reflected in the study by Matsuoka \textit{et al.} (2013), who found a negative correlation between elliptical form and ALP activity across 666 morphometric features. Notably, their study observed significant morphological changes after 7 days, but significant changes in ALP and calcium deposition only after 14 and 21 days, respectively, suggesting that extending our osteogenic assay beyond 8 days would increase statistical power. Furthermore, as ALP expression peaks during early commitment and subsequently declines, incorporating later-stage markers of osteoblast maturation- such as calcium deposition and osteopontin- would allow for a more holistic qualification of each clone's osteogenic potential.

In a contrasting interpretation to Yourek \textit{et al}'s study, clone B is unlikely to be a post-differentiation pre-osteoblast, as both clones had similar basal ALP levels, indicating equal non-commitment at the start of the experiment; culturing in basal expansion medium prior to the assay would have ensured any pre-existing osteogenic commitment progressed to Osterix-mediated maturation before the assay commenced. Clone B is also unlikely to be more senescent: hTERT excludes telomere-related senescence, and while stress-induced or oncogene-induced senescence remains possible (Nelson \textit{et al.}, 2014), clone B's continued proliferation argues against growth arrest characteristic of senescent hMSCs (Anconelli \textit{et al.}, 2025). Future work is needed to qualify clone B's potency using lineage-specific stains (Oil Red O for adipogenesis; GAG/PG for chondrogenesis) and to quantify senescence via telomere length qPCR (Samsonraj \textit{et al.}, 2013).

The data therefore suggest that clone B is a less potent, but equally non-senescent and non-committed, hMSC. The most parsimonious explanation is that clone A's parallel F-actin architecture generates greater cytoskeletal tension via RhoA/ROCK-myosin contraction, promoting YAP/TAZ nuclear translocation and thus greater RUNX2 upregulation and ALP expression (Dupont \textit{et al.}, 2011), whereas clone B's radial actin organisation dissipates this tension, reducing mechanotransductive signalling. An alternative, non-mutually-exclusive explanation is that clone B has lower glucocorticoid receptor expression, reducing dex-mediated RhoA/ROCK activation (Du \textit{et al.}, 2009). Both hypotheses could be tested through YAP/TAZ immunofluorescence and GR quantification via flow cytometry, respectively.

Our findings, plus the stated additional research, may aid the development of hMSC-based therapies, where variability in potency batch-to-batch remains a principle barrier to consistent clinical results (Costa \textit{et al.}, 2021; Silverman \textit{et al.}, 2019). Hernigou \textit{et al.} (2005) show that the success of percutaneous bone marrow grafting for tibial nonunion is dependent on the number of osteogenic progenitors injected (p < 0.01). This demonstrates the vital importance of high potency on the therapy prognosis, yet as of current, potency assays are destructive, time-consuming, or require animal models (Scott \textit{et al.}, 2012), meaning that low potency clonal lines are often identified only after significant investment of time and resources. Our study demonstrated that label-free QPI can distinguish high from low osteogenically potent cells based on morphological and movement-based properties. Combined with machine learning- as demonstrated by Mai \textit{et al.} (2023), whose convolutional neural network identified high-potency hMSCs from morphology alone with 95.72% accuracy- ptychographic QPI could serve as a rapid, minimally invasive (Rindy and Chambers, 2023) screening tool for selecting high-potency clones prior to clinical use.

In conclusion, this study demonstrates that hMSC clones that differ in osteogenic potential exhibit distinct and separable morphokinetic characteristics which are detectable by label-free ptychographic QPI. This supports the broader idea that differentiation potential is not solely a molecular property, but is manifested in cytoskeletal architecture and cell morphology and movement. The remaining challenge is identifying whether this correlation holds across different donors, which would determine whether morphokinetic profiling is a clinically viable method of predicting hMSC potency.

\textbf{Word count: n (https://wordcounter.net)}

# References

Adam, N. et al. (2025). hTERT Increases TRF2 to Induce Telomere Compaction and Extend Cell Replicative Lifespan. Aging Cell, 24 (8), p.e70105. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1111/acel.70105.

Aldahmash, A. et al. (2012). Human stromal (mesenchymal) stem cells: basic biology and current clinical use for tissue regeneration. Annals of Saudi Medicine, 32 (1), pp.68–77. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.5144/0256-4947.2012.68.

Ambriz, X., De Lanerolle, P. and Ambrosio, J. R. (2018). The Mechanobiology of the Actin Cytoskeleton in Stem Cells during Differentiation and Interaction with Biomaterials. Stem Cells International, 2018, pp.1–11. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1155/2018/2891957.

Anconelli, L. et al. (2025). Ptychographic analysis of human bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cell morphology: The impact of cell senescence. Journal of Microscopy, 300 (2), pp.227–233. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1111/jmi.70003.

Bertolo, A. et al. (2015). In Vitro Cell Motility as a Potential Mesenchymal Stem Cell Marker for Multipotency. Stem Cells Translational Medicine, 4 (1), pp.84–90. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.5966/sctm.2014-0156.

Costa, L. A. et al. (2021). Functional heterogeneity of mesenchymal stem cells from natural niches to culture conditions: implications for further clinical uses. Cellular and molecular life sciences: CMLS, 78 (2), pp.447–467. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1007/s00018-020-03600-0.

Du, J. et al. (2009). Flow cytometry analysis of glucocorticoid receptor expression and binding in steroid-sensitive and steroid-resistant patients with systemic lupus erythematosus. Arthritis Research & Therapy, 11 (4), p.R108. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1186/ar2763.

Dupont, S. et al. (2011). Role of YAP/TAZ in mechanotransduction. Nature, 474 (7350), pp.179–183. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1038/nature10137.

Hernigou, Ph. et al. (2005). Percutaneous Autologous Bone-Marrow Grafting for NonunionsInfluence of the Number and Concentration of Progenitor Cells. The Journal of Bone and Joint Surgery (American), 87 (7), p.1430. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.2106/JBJS.D.02215.

Hsuan, Y. C. et al. (2016). Mesenchymal stem cell‐based treatments for stroke, neural trauma, and heat stroke. Brain and Behavior, 6 (10), p.e00526. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1002/brb3.526.

Licas J. Rindy and Aaron R. Chambers. (2023). Bone Marrow Aspiration and Biopsy. StatPearls [Online]. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559232/ [Accessed 5 May 2026].

Liu, Y. and Tang, S. C. W. (2016). Recent Progress in Stem Cell Therapy for Diabetic Nephropathy. Kidney Diseases, 2 (1), pp.20–27. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1159/000441913.

Mai, M. et al. (2023). Morphology-based deep learning approach for predicting adipogenic and osteogenic differentiation of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs). Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology, 11, p.1329840. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.3389/fcell.2023.1329840.

Marrison, J. et al. (2013). Ptychography – a label free, high-contrast imaging technique for live cells using quantitative phase information. Scientific Reports, 3 (1), p.2369. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1038/srep02369.

Matsuoka, F. et al. (2013). Morphology-Based Prediction of Osteogenic Differentiation Potential of Human Mesenchymal Stem Cells. Pelacho, B. (Ed). PLoS ONE, 8 (2), p.e55082. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0055082.

Nelson, D. M. et al. (2014). A comparison of oncogene-induced senescence and replicative senescence: implications for tumor suppression and aging. Age, 36 (3), p.9637. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1007/s11357-014-9637-0.

R Core Team. (2026). R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing. Vienna: R Foundation for Statistical Computin. [Online]. Available at: https://www.R-project.org/.

Samsonraj, R. M. et al. (2013). Telomere length analysis of human mesenchymal stem cells by quantitative PCR. Gene, 519 (2), pp.348–355. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1016/j.gene.2013.01.039.

Scott, M. A. et al. (2012). Brief review of models of ectopic bone formation. Stem Cells and Development, 21 (5), pp.655–667. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1089/scd.2011.0517.

Silverman, L. I. et al. (2019). Identifying and Managing Sources of Variability in Cell Therapy Manufacturing and Clinical Trials. Regenerative Engineering and Translational Medicine, 5 (4), pp.354–361. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1007/s40883-019-00129-y.

Smith, L. et al. (2025). Evaluating Strategies to Assess the Differentiation Potential of Human Pluripotent Stem Cells: A Review, Analysis and Call for Innovation. Stem Cell Reviews and Reports, 21 (1), pp.107–125. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1007/s12015-024-10793-5.

Trombi, L. et al. (2016). Mesenchymal Stromal Cell Culture and Delivery in Autologous Conditions: A Smart Approach for Orthopedic Applications. Journal of Visualized Experiments: JoVE, (118), p.54845. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.3791/54845.

Yourek, G., Hussain, M. A. and Mao, J. J. (2007). Cytoskeletal changes of mesenchymal stem cells during differentiation. ASAIO journal, 53 (2), pp.219–228. [Online]. Available at: doi:10.1097/MAT.0b013e31802deb2d.
